clean_story:
Disorder in data. Efficiency through chaos.

Entropy—borrowed from physics—measures disorder. In databases, it’s the unpredictability of how data spreads, clumps, or decays over time.

A database starts neat: rows lined like books on a shelf. But queries scramble them. Inserts split pages. Deletes leave gaps. Without control, entropy turns searches into treasure hunts—slow, random, costly. Like a library where books reshelve themselves overnight, but only by color, not title.

The fix? Indexes (like card catalogs) and defragmentation (re-shelving books by order). Yet even these fight entropy. Hash functions distribute data "randomly" to avoid clumps—but randomness is entropy’s cousin. Compression shrinks storage but adds decoding steps, trading space for speed.

High-entropy systems (e.g., NoSQL) embrace chaos: they scatter data across nodes, knowing retrieval will be messy but fast. Low-entropy ones (e.g., SQL) enforce rigid schemas, sacrificing flexibility for predictability.

Three related subjects are data decay over time, query optimization tricks, and how heat death mirrors database bloat.
